claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Remember, eligibility is determined separately for each child.

First, could you tell me: **Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm asking about. My son Alex is 6 years old and he lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know that Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Since Alex is 6 years old (under 16), he meets the age requirement. Now I need to ask a few more questions about your circumstances.

**Are you currently responsible for Alex?** By this I mean, does he normally live with you and you're responsible for his care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives with me and I'm responsible for him - I take care of him day to day.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. Now I need to ask about your residence status.

**Are you currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're here in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand a bit more about your immigration and residence status.

**Do you have the right to reside in the UK?** This could include being a British citizen, having settled status, pre-settled status, or having another immigration status that gives you the right to live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have the right to reside in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about Alex specifically:

**Is Alex in receipt of any benefits in his own right?** For example, Income Support, Jobseeker's Allowance, Employment and Support Allowance, or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, that's fine. Next question:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?** (At 6 years old, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 6, so he's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming. Now I need to ask:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** For example, Alex's other parent or another person?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. Now I need to understand Alex's living situation a bit more:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations:**
- In hospital
- In local authority care
- Living with foster carers
- Or does he live normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives normally at home with me - none of those special situations apply.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of why you qualify:
- Alex is 6 years old (under 16, so he's a qualifying child)
- He's not receiving any benefits in his own right
- He's not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK and have the right to reside here
- You're responsible for Alex and he lives with you
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- He's living at home with you (not in hospital or care)
- The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance

**Next steps to claim:**
1. You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. You'll need to provide proof of identity for yourself and Alex
3. You'll need Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate

**Important to note:** If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge, which means you might have to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return. However, you can still claim it - and many people do even if they have to pay it back, as it protects your National Insurance record and future State Pension.

Do you have any questions about claiming or the process?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's great news, thank you so much for explaining everything! No, I think you've covered everything I needed to know. I'll get started with the application.
